name: Donmez, Birsen
recent_1: Wang, J., Li, W., Wang, Z., Ayas, S., Donmez, B., He, D., & Wu, K. (2026). DrowsyDG-Phys: Generalizable driver drowsiness estimation in conditional automated vehicles using physiological signals. Accident Analysis & Prevention, 228, 108407. DOI: 10.1016/j.aap.2026.108407
recent_1_synopsis: This paper develops a machine-learning approach that estimates driver drowsiness from physiological signals while generalizing across drivers and conditions in conditionally automated vehicles. It addresses the practical problem that drowsiness detection models often fail to transfer to new individuals, advancing robust driver-state monitoring for automated driving.
recent_2: DeGuzman, C. A., Demir, B., Ayas, S., & Donmez, B. (2026). A review of in-vehicle touchscreen safety and usability. Transportation Research Interdisciplinary Perspectives, 36, 101873. DOI: 10.1016/j.trip.2026.101873
recent_2_synopsis: A rapid review synthesizing 73 studies on the safety and usability of in-vehicle touchscreens, finding that touchscreens generally perform worse than physical or voice controls and that no consolidated design guidelines exist. It frames an evidence base intended to inform future interface design standards for distraction-resistant in-vehicle systems.
recent_3: Ayas, S., He, D., & Donmez, B. (2026). Driver state classification: Identifying high cognitive load and drowsiness through driver performance and physiology. IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems, 27(1), 1105-1115. DOI: 10.1109/tits.2025.3633499
recent_3_synopsis: This study builds classifiers that distinguish high cognitive load from drowsiness by combining driving-performance measures with physiological signals. Disentangling these two impairing states is important because they call for different interventions, and the work informs the design of adaptive driver monitoring systems.
seminal_1: Donmez, B., Boyle, L. N., & Lee, J. D. (2007). Safety implications of providing real-time feedback to distracted drivers. Accident Analysis & Prevention, 39(3), 581-590. DOI: 10.1016/j.aap.2006.10.003
seminal_1_synopsis: This driving-simulator study demonstrated that real-time feedback keyed to drivers' off-road glances can reduce engagement in in-vehicle distractions and improve attention to the road across age groups. With roughly 320 citations (Google Scholar), it is her most-cited work and a foundational demonstration that behavioral feedback can mitigate distraction in real time.
seminal_2: Donmez, B., Boyle, L. N., & Lee, J. D. (2006). The impact of distraction mitigation strategies on driving performance. Human Factors, 48(4), 785-804. DOI: 10.1518/001872006779166415
seminal_2_synopsis: Published in Human Factors, this paper evaluated how different distraction-mitigation strategies affect driving performance and how drivers accept them, distinguishing advising from locking-out approaches. With roughly 175 citations (Google Scholar), it is foundational for the design of adaptive, driver-acceptable distraction countermeasures.
expertise_overview: Donmez studies driver distraction, attention, and human-automation interaction, with a focus on how feedback and monitoring can guide safer driver behavior. Her work spans the detection and classification of impairing states such as visual-manual and cognitive distraction, drowsiness, and high workload, often combining driving-performance metrics with physiological signals and machine learning. She investigates anticipatory driving, takeover and disengagement in partially and conditionally automated vehicles, and the design of in-vehicle interfaces (including touchscreens and feedback displays). Methodologically she relies on driving simulators, naturalistic and on-road studies, eye-tracking, statistical modeling of crash and behavioral data, and questionnaire-based individual-differences research. Application domains extend beyond driving to interruptions in intensive care, supervisory control of unmanned vehicles, and decision support for transportation safety. A recurring theme is understanding individual differences in susceptibility to distraction and using that understanding to design effective, acceptable countermeasures.
notes: Identified with high confidence via Google Scholar (user=mmsNZdoAAAAJ), University of Toronto MIE/HFAST/UTTRI faculty pages, and ResearchGate; all eight anchor hf_papers in Human Factors match this profile (University of Toronto, NSERC Canada Research Chair in Human Factors and Transportation). Citation counts from Google Scholar as of June 2026 (total ~4,600; h-index 39). All cited DOIs verified via Crossref (HTTP 200, matching titles) on 2026-06-17: recent papers 10.1016/j.aap.2026.108407, 10.1016/j.trip.2026.101873, 10.1109/tits.2025.3633499 (TITS article carries a 2026 issue date in Crossref though listed as 2025 online); seminal papers 10.1016/j.aap.2006.10.003 and 10.1518/001872006779166415. No fabricated DOIs; no unverifiable citations.
